rows.v: The defining instrument of rock and pop — a solid-body guitar whose strings are turned into sound electronically.
Endlessly versatile, from clean and shimmering to screaming distortion, shaped entirely by amps and effects pedals.
Strings are plucked or strummed; magnetic pickups convert their vibration into a signal sent to an amplifier.
Every rock riff and solo; the wail of Hendrix, the crunch of any guitar band.
The hollow-bodied guitar that needs no amplifier — the workhorse of folk, pop, and singer-songwriters.
Warm, woody, and resonant, equally suited to gentle fingerpicking and bright rhythmic strumming.
Strings (steel or nylon) are strummed or picked; the hollow body amplifies the sound acoustically.
The campfire singalong; the intimate backing of any singer-songwriter.
The low-end backbone of nearly every band — it locks together the rhythm and the harmony.
Deep, round, and groovy, felt as much as heard, sitting between the drums and the rest of the band.
Usually four thick strings plucked with fingers or a pick; like an electric guitar but lower and longer.
The groove under any funk, rock, or pop song; the walking line in jazz.
A small, four-stringed Hawaiian cousin of the guitar with a bright, cheerful voice.
Light, sweet, and jangly, instantly evoking a sunny, breezy, feel-good mood.
Four nylon strings strummed or picked; tiny and easy to learn, which fueled its huge popularity.
Indie-pop singalongs; anything aiming for a warm, carefree, tropical feel.
A horizontal steel guitar played on a stand with foot pedals and knee levers — the crying voice of country music.
Smooth, swooping, and vocal, sliding fluidly between notes with a weeping, shimmering quality.
The player slides a steel bar along the strings while pedals and levers bend the pitch of individual strings.
The signature weeping sound of classic country; lush pedal-steel textures in Americana and pop.
An electronic keyboard that generates and shapes sound from scratch — the foundation of electronic music.
Almost limitless: warm pads, sharp leads, deep basses, and sounds that resemble no acoustic instrument at all.
Oscillators create raw waveforms that are shaped by filters and envelopes; controlled by a keyboard or knobs.
The pulsing basslines and soaring leads of synth-pop, EDM, and film scores.
An electromechanical keyboard with a warm, bell-like tone — the soulful sound of 1970s funk and soul.
Mellow, warm, and slightly bell-like, with a characteristic 'bark' when played hard.
Keys strike metal tines or reeds whose vibration is picked up electrically, like a cross between a piano and a guitar.
The lush keys of soul, funk, and jazz fusion; the iconic Rhodes and Wurlitzer sounds.
An electric organ with a swirling, soulful sound, made famous in gospel, jazz, and rock.
Rich, warm, and swirling, especially through a rotating Leslie speaker that gives it its signature throb.
Drawbars blend electronic tones; a foot pedalboard plays bass while both hands work two keyboards.
The soulful swell of gospel and blues; the churning organ of classic rock.
A portable, bellows-driven keyboard with reeds — the heart of folk music across many cultures.
Reedy, rich, and full, capable of both melody and accompaniment at once, with a distinctive breathing quality.
The player squeezes the bellows to push air over reeds while pressing keys or buttons on each side.
French café music, polka, tango, Cajun, and folk traditions across the world.
A small handheld keyboard you blow into — part wind instrument, part piano.
Bright, reedy, and breathy, like a cross between a harmonica and a toy organ, with a charming, intimate tone.
The player blows through a mouthpiece while pressing piano-style keys that open reeds.
Reggae and dub textures; lo-fi and indie tracks reaching for a warm, casual sound.
The assembled set of drums and cymbals played by one person — the engine of rock, pop, and jazz.
Powerful and driving, combining the thump of bass drum, snap of snare, and shimmer of cymbals into one groove.
Played seated, using both hands on drums and cymbals and both feet on the bass drum and hi-hat pedals.
The beat behind virtually every rock, pop, and jazz recording.
A wooden box drum the player sits on and slaps — a portable rhythm section in a single instrument.
Punchy and warm, with a deep bass tone in the center and a sharp snare-like snap at the edges.
The player sits on top and strikes the front face with hands, getting bass and snare sounds from different spots.
Acoustic and unplugged sessions; flamenco, where it became a staple.
Tall, narrow hand drums from Afro-Cuban music, usually played in pairs or threes.
Warm, resonant, and rhythmic, ranging from deep open tones to sharp slaps.
Played standing or seated with the bare hands, using different strokes for distinct pitches and tones.
Latin music, salsa, and Afro-Cuban grooves; percussion layers in funk and pop.
A rope-tuned, goblet-shaped West African hand drum with an enormous range of tones.
Hugely dynamic, from a deep resonant bass to a cracking high slap, all from one drumhead.
Played with bare hands between the knees or on a stand; hand position and strike point change the tone.
West African drumming circles; world-music and fusion percussion.
An electronic device that creates and sequences drum sounds — the heartbeat of hip-hop, techno, and pop.
From punchy synthetic kicks and snares to crisp electronic hi-hats; defined the sound of entire genres.
The user programs a pattern step by step, or plays pads, and the machine loops the beat back.
The iconic beats of hip-hop and electronic music; classic boxes like the Roland 808 and 909.
A small hand frame fitted with jingling metal discs — one of the simplest and most universal rhythm instruments.
Bright, jingling, and shimmering, adding sparkle and drive to a backbeat.
Shaken, tapped against the hand or hip, or struck to mark the rhythm.
The shimmer in gospel and pop choruses; a staple of folk and rock percussion.
A bright, twangy string instrument with a drum-like body — the signature sound of bluegrass and folk.
Bright, sharp, and percussive, with a distinctive ringing twang that cuts through any acoustic band.
Strings are plucked over a round, drumhead-covered body; bluegrass uses a fast three-finger 'rolling' style.
Bluegrass breakdowns; the rolling twang in folk and Americana.
A small, round-backed string instrument with eight strings in four pairs, played with a pick.
Bright, crisp, and shimmering, with a fast tremolo that lets it sustain a singing melody.
Played with a pick; the doubled strings give it a rich, ringing tone, often rapidly tremolo-picked.
Bluegrass and folk; the lyrical mandolin lines in Italian and Celtic music.
The very same instrument as the violin, but played in folk, country, and bluegrass styles.
Lively, raw, and rhythmic, leaning into a driving, danceable energy rather than classical polish.
Held and bowed exactly like a violin, but with folk techniques — drones, slides, and rhythmic 'chops.'
Country and bluegrass hoedowns; Celtic and Appalachian dance tunes.
A tiny handheld reed instrument you play with your breath — pocket-sized, expressive, and soulful.
Wailing, breathy, and vocal, from gentle folk melodies to crying, bent blues notes.
The player breathes in and out through holes; each draws or blows a reed. 'Bending' breath alters the pitch.
The lonesome wail of the blues; folk and country, from Dylan to Little Walter.
A resonator guitar with a metal cone inside that makes it louder and gives it a distinctive metallic ring.
Bright, metallic, and resonant, with a sustained, slightly nasal twang, often played with a slide.
Strings are plucked over a metal resonator cone; often played flat on the lap with a steel bar (slide).
Bluegrass and Americana; the slide-guitar growl of country blues.
A long-necked Indian string instrument with sympathetic strings that give it a shimmering, droning resonance.
Rich, buzzing, and hypnotic, with a complex ringing wash from extra strings that vibrate on their own.
Plucked with a wire pick; the player bends the strings sideways across curved frets to slide between notes.
Indian classical music; the psychedelic shimmer it brought to 1960s rock like the Beatles.
A goblet-shaped West African hand drum, central to traditional drumming and now used worldwide.
Dynamic and expressive, with deep bass tones and sharp, cracking slaps from a single skin.
Played with bare hands; different strokes and hand positions produce its full range of tones.
Traditional West African ensembles; drum circles and world-fusion music everywhere.
A tuned percussion instrument hammered from an oil drum — the national instrument of Trinidad and Tobago.
Bright, shimmering, and bell-like, capable of full melodies and harmonies, with a sunny, tropical character.
The player strikes different hammered sections of the concave drum surface, each tuned to a note, with rubber-tipped sticks.
Caribbean calypso and soca; the unmistakable sound of a steel band at Carnival.
A reed instrument powered by air squeezed from a bag, famous for its continuous droning sound.
Loud, piercing, and stirring, with a constant background drone under the melody and no gaps between notes.
The player blows into a bag (or pumps bellows) and squeezes it to push air through the melody pipe and drones.
Scottish and Irish traditional music; the haunting solo pipes of military and ceremonial occasions.
A long wooden tube from Aboriginal Australia, one of the world's oldest wind instruments.
Deep, droning, and resonant, with a hypnotic buzzing pulse and rich overtones.
The player buzzes their lips into one end and uses 'circular breathing' to sustain the drone without pausing.
Aboriginal Australian ceremony and music; ambient and world-fusion textures.
A thumb piano — metal tines mounted on a wooden board, plucked with the thumbs.
Sweet, gentle, and music-box-like, with a soft, soothing, bell-like tone.
The player holds it in both hands and plucks the metal tines with their thumbs; tine length sets the pitch.
African mbira traditions; calming, lo-fi, and ambient music.
A two-stringed Chinese bowed instrument with a remarkably expressive, voice-like tone.
Soulful, singing, and melancholy, capable of expressive slides and vibrato close to the human voice.
Played upright on the lap; the bow hair is fixed between the two strings, and there's no fingerboard to press against.
Chinese classical and folk music; evocative film scores reaching for an East Asian sound.
Record players used as instruments — the foundation of hip-hop DJing and turntablism.
Percussive and rhythmic, turning vinyl into scratches, stabs, and beats manipulated by hand.
The DJ moves the record by hand against the needle to 'scratch,' and blends two turntables through a mixer.
The scratching and cutting at the birth of hip-hop; the art of turntablism.
A device or software that records snippets of sound and lets you replay them as an instrument.
Anything at all — a chopped soul record, a vocal, a single drum hit — replayed, pitched, and looped.
Sounds are recorded ('sampled'), assigned to pads or keys, and triggered and manipulated in performance.
The chopped-up loops at the heart of hip-hop and electronic music.
Digital Audio Workstation — the software that has become the modern studio and instrument in one.
Everything: it records, sequences, synthesizes, and mixes, often replacing a whole room of equipment.
Producers arrange MIDI and audio on a screen timeline, layering virtual instruments and effects.
How nearly all modern music is made; tools like Ableton Live, Logic, and FL Studio.
A keyboard or pad device that sends control signals (not sound) to software instruments.
Itself silent — it triggers whatever virtual instrument it's connected to, from pianos to synths to drums.
The player presses keys or pads; MIDI data tells the computer which notes and how hard to play them.
The hands-on control surface behind most modern electronic production.
A device that fuses a voice with a synthesizer, producing the classic 'robot voice' sound.
Robotic, harmonic, and otherworldly, turning sung or spoken words into chords of synthetic tone.
The player sings into a mic while playing chords; the voice's character is imposed onto the synth sound.
The robotic vocals of electro and funk; acts like Daft Punk and Kraftwerk.
The original and most universal instrument — the human voice, carried by every culture on Earth.
Infinitely expressive, the model every other instrument is measured against, capable of pitch, rhythm, and pure emotion.
Produced by air from the lungs vibrating the vocal cords, shaped by the throat, mouth, and lips into pitch and words.
Every song ever sung; from opera to rock to a cappella harmony.
Vocal percussion — imitating drum machines and instruments using only the mouth, voice, and breath.
Punchy and percussive, mimicking kicks, snares, hi-hats, scratches, and even basslines with startling realism.
The performer uses lips, tongue, and breath to imitate drum and electronic sounds, often into a microphone.
Hip-hop and a cappella; standalone beatbox performance and battles.
Making rhythm with the body itself — clapping, stomping, snapping, and slapping.
Earthy and immediate, an entire groove built from the sounds the body can make with no instrument at all.
Performers clap hands, stamp feet, slap thighs and chest, and snap fingers in coordinated patterns.
Stomp-style shows; the clap-and-stomp groove of songs like 'We Will Rock You.'
